sources.citation: Pitkin, H. F. (1967). The Concept of Representation. University of California Press.
sources.relevance: Foundational theoretical work defining political representation, including descriptive, substantive, and symbolic dimensions that underpin survey measures of perceived representativeness.
sources.key_finding: Representation is a multidimensional concept where citizens' perceptions of being represented depend on both symbolic identification and substantive policy responsiveness.

sources.citation: Moreno, A. (2003). El votante mexicano: Democracia, actitudes políticas y conducta electoral. Fondo de Cultura Económica.
sources.relevance: Analyzes Mexican voters' attitudes toward political institutions and elected officials, including trust and perceived representation across government levels.
sources.key_finding: Mexican citizens' perceptions of representation vary significantly by government level, with local officials often perceived as more responsive but less capable than federal ones.

sources.citation: Hagopian, F. (2005). Government performance, political representation, and public perceptions of contemporary democracy in Latin America. In F. Hagopian & S. Mainwaring (Eds.), The Third Wave of Democratization in Latin America. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Examines how citizens in Latin American democracies evaluate political representation across institutional levels and party systems.
sources.key_finding: Perceived representativeness in Latin America is strongly shaped by government performance evaluations and the degree to which parties articulate citizen interests.

sources.citation: Cleary, M. R., & Stokes, S. C. (2006). Democracy and the Culture of Skepticism: Political Trust in Argentina and Mexico. Russell Sage Foundation.
sources.relevance: Directly studies how Mexican and Argentine citizens evaluate whether elected officials represent their interests, linking skepticism to institutional performance.
sources.key_finding: Citizens in Mexico use performance-based rather than purely cultural criteria to assess whether officials represent them, with significant variation across municipal, state, and federal levels.

sources.citation: Díaz-Cayeros, A. (2006). Federalism, Fiscal Authority, and Centralization in Latin America. Cambridge University Press.
sources.relevance: Directly examines fiscal federalism in Mexico, analyzing how revenue sharing and service delivery responsibilities are allocated across government levels and how citizens perceive these arrangements.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's fiscal federalism has been characterized by centralized revenue collection with decentralized spending, creating mismatches between citizen expectations and government capacity at subnational levels.

sources.citation: Flamand, L. (2006). El juego de la distribución de recursos en un sistema federal: La influencia del gobierno dividido verticalmente en la asignación de fondos federales a los estados mexicanos. Política y Gobierno, 13(2), 315-359.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how federal resource distribution to Mexican states is shaped by political dynamics, directly relevant to citizen preferences about fiscal allocation.
sources.key_finding: Vertical divided government significantly influences the distribution of federal funds to states, suggesting that partisan dynamics shape the fiscal federalism outcomes citizens experience.

sources.citation: Oates, W. E. (1999). An essay on fiscal federalism. Journal of Economic Literature, 37(3), 1120-1149.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical framework for understanding citizen preferences about decentralization of public services and fiscal responsibilities.
sources.key_finding: Decentralization of service delivery can improve welfare by matching public goods provision to local preferences, but efficiency gains depend on local government capacity and accountability.

sources.citation: Cabrero Mendoza, E. (2004). Capacidades institucionales en gobiernos subnacionales de México: ¿Un obstáculo para la descentralización fiscal? Gestión y Política Pública, 13(3), 753-784.
sources.relevance: Examines whether Mexican subnational governments have the institutional capacity to manage decentralized fiscal and service responsibilities, directly relevant to citizen preferences about which level should manage services.
sources.key_finding: Significant heterogeneity in institutional capacity across Mexican municipalities limits the effectiveness of fiscal decentralization and shapes citizen satisfaction with local service delivery.

causal_relationships.related_construct: tax morale / willingness to pay taxes

sources.citation: Serna de la Garza, J. M. (2008). El sistema federal mexicano: Un análisis jurídico. UNAM, Instituto de Investigaciones Jurídicas.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the legal architecture of Mexican federalism, including the tension between uniform national legislation and state-level legal autonomy across policy domains.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's federal system has historically oscillated between centralizing legal frameworks and decentralized state autonomy, with recent reforms tending toward national harmonization in areas like security and transparency.

sources.citation: Riker, W. H. (1964). Federalism: Origin, Operation, Significance. Little, Brown and Company.
sources.relevance: Classic theoretical treatment of federalism that addresses the fundamental tension between legal uniformity and state-level variation as a core dimension of federal governance.
sources.key_finding: The degree of legal centralization in federal systems reflects the balance of power between national and subnational governments, with citizen preferences playing a mediating role in democratic federations.

sources.citation: López Ayllón, S., & Merino, M. (2010). La rendición de cuentas en México: Perspectivas y retos. In M. Merino, S. López Ayllón, & G. Cejudo (Eds.), La estructura de la rendición de cuentas en México. UNAM-CIDE.
sources.relevance: Discusses the push for uniform national laws on transparency and accountability in Mexico, directly relevant to the information access and corruption items in this construct.
sources.key_finding: The creation of uniform national transparency and anti-corruption frameworks was driven partly by citizen demand for consistent standards across states with varying institutional quality.

causal_relationships.related_construct: perceived corruption levels across government levels
perceived rule of law / justice system effectiveness

gap_justification: While there is substantial legal scholarship on the structure of Mexican federalism, there is a notable gap in empirical public opinion research measuring citizen preferences for legal uniformity versus state-specific laws across multiple policy domains simultaneously. Most existing studies focus on elite or institutional perspectives rather than mass attitudes. This construct fills a critical gap by capturing the normative dimension of centralization from the citizen perspective, revealing whether Mexicans prefer consistent national standards or locally tailored legal frameworks. Understanding these preferences is essential for evaluating the democratic legitimacy of recent centralizing legal reforms in Mexico, such as the creation of national codes for criminal procedure and anti-corruption frameworks.

sources.citation: Moloeznik, M. P. (2006). The challenges to Mexico in times of political change. Crime, Law and Social Change, 46(4-5), 169-187.
sources.relevance: Examines the governance challenges of public security in Mexico, including debates over centralized versus decentralized police forces and intergovernmental coordination.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's fragmented security governance across municipal, state, and federal levels has been a major obstacle to effective crime control, fueling debates about police centralization.

sources.citation: Sabet, D. (2012). Police Reform in Mexico: Informal Politics and the Challenge of Institutional Change. Stanford University Press.
sources.relevance: Directly addresses citizen and institutional preferences regarding police organization, centralization, and the distribution of security responsibilities across government levels in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Efforts to reform Mexican police are complicated by overlapping jurisdictions and weak municipal capacity, with citizens increasingly favoring federal intervention in local security matters.

sources.citation: Bailey, J. (2014). The Politics of Crime in Mexico: Democratic Governance in a Security Trap. Lynne Rienner Publishers.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the political dynamics of security governance in Mexico, including public opinion on which government level should handle security and the effectiveness of intergovernmental coordination.
sources.key_finding: Mexico is caught in a 'security trap' where weak institutions at multiple government levels reinforce each other, and citizen preferences for centralized security reflect low trust in local authorities.

sources.citation: Vilalta, C. (2016). Does the Mexican war on organized crime mediate the impact of fear of crime on daily routines? Crime & Delinquency, 62(11), 1483-1504.
sources.relevance: Examines how the federal government's security strategy affects citizen perceptions and behaviors, relevant to understanding preferences about which level should manage security.
sources.key_finding: Federal security interventions shape citizen fear of crime and daily routines, suggesting that government-level security governance decisions have direct impacts on public life.

causal_relationships.related_construct: trust in police and security institutions

sources.citation: Verba, S., Schlozman, K. L., & Brady, H. E. (1995). Voice and Equality: Civic Voluntarism in American Politics. Harvard University Press.
sources.relevance: Foundational work on political engagement that establishes the theoretical and empirical framework for measuring political interest, news consumption, and perceived relevance of politics as components of civic engagement.
sources.key_finding: Political interest and engagement are driven by resources (time, money, skills), recruitment networks, and psychological engagement, with interest in politics being a key predictor of all forms of participation.

sources.citation: Moreno, A., & Méndez, P. (2007). Getting to know the Mexican voter. In J. I. Domínguez, C. Lawson, & A. Moreno (Eds.), Consolidating Mexico's Democracy: The 2006 Presidential Campaign in Comparative Perspective. Johns Hopkins University Press.
sources.relevance: Directly examines political interest, media consumption, and engagement among Mexican citizens in the context of democratic consolidation.
sources.key_finding: Political interest in Mexico is stratified by education and socioeconomic status, with media consumption patterns significantly shaping political knowledge and engagement.

sources.citation: Buendía, J., & Somuano, F. (2003). Participación electoral en nuevas democracias: La elección presidencial de 2000 en México. Política y Gobierno, 10(2), 289-323.
sources.relevance: Analyzes determinants of political participation in Mexico, including political interest and perceived relevance of politics as predictors of electoral engagement.
sources.key_finding: Political interest is one of the strongest predictors of voter turnout in Mexico, mediating the effects of socioeconomic variables on electoral participation.

sources.citation: Prior, M. (2010). You've either got it or you don't? The stability of political interest over the life course. Journal of Politics, 72(3), 747-766.
sources.relevance: Examines the stability and determinants of political interest as a core attitudinal construct, relevant to understanding the measurement properties of political interest items.
sources.key_finding: Political interest is remarkably stable over the life course, suggesting it functions as a deep-seated orientation rather than a situational response.
